summary: Gruber provides a candid update on his experiment of writing Daring Fireball full-time, revealing he's at about 50% of breaking even with revenue split evenly between Deck advertising and direct reader support. He announces a free RSS feeds week as a promotional strategy to convert more readers into paying members, comparing it to free HBO weekends. He also mentions site licensing options for group memberships. The post is a transparent look at the economics of independent blogging in 2006, framed with Gruber's characteristic mix of earnestness and humor.
key_points: Revenue is split almost exactly 50-50 between The Deck advertising network and direct reader contributions (memberships and t-shirts)
Gruber values reader-funded income more than advertising dollars as a writer, finding direct reader support 'indescribably satisfying'
The free feeds promotion is a strategic trial period to hook non-members on RSS feeds that 80% of existing members already use
Independent blogging funded significantly by voluntary reader contributions was still rare in 2006, with Kottke's micropatronage being a notable but short-lived experiment
money_quotes: As a businessman, I ought to be thinking that every dollar is as good as another. But I'm not a businessman, I'm a writer, and as a writer there's something indescribably satisfying about support that comes directly from my readers.
It's no longer all that novel to "go pro" with one's weblog, but there aren't many who are doing it based so significantly on voluntary contributions from readers.

Think of it like when there's free HBO for a weekend — if you like the shows, you pay to subscribe when the free period is over. Or, think of it like when a drug dealer gives out a free sample to get you hooked. No, wait, don't think of it like that.
The answer, in a nut, is "pretty good".
Those of you working at YouTube, I'm looking in your direction.
key_insight: In the early days of professional blogging, Gruber bet that direct reader support could sustain independent writing — valuing the writer-reader relationship over pure advertising revenue.
